title: Building and Running
description: Understand how Go compiles and executes code.

slides.title: Compilation 🏗️
slides.content:
## From Text to Machine Code

Computers don't actually understand words like `func` or `fmt`. They only understand binary (1s and 0s).

Go is a **compiled language**. This means before you run it, a tool (the compiler) translates your human-readable Go code into lightning-fast machine code that your computer's brain (the CPU) can execute directly.

quizData.question: What is the process of translating human-readable Go code into machine code called?
quizData.explanation: Go is a compiled language, making it very fast at runtime.

slides.title: go build 🔨
slides.content:
## Crafting the Executable

If you have a file named `main.go`, and you want to share it with your friends as a real application, you run:

`go build main.go`

This creates a standalone executable file (like a `.exe` on Windows). You can send this file to a friend, and they can run it **even if they don't have Go installed!**

quizData.question: If your friend doesn't have Go installed on their computer, can they still run your compiled Go program?
quizData.explanation: `go build` creates a binary that bundles everything needed to run the app standalone.

slides.title: go run 🏃
slides.content:
## Quick Testing

While developing, running `go build` and then running the file is too slow.

Instead, developers use:
`go run main.go`

This command silently compiles your code in a hidden temporary folder, runs it immediately, and shows you the result. It's the perfect way to test your code quickly!

quizData.question: Which command compiles AND immediately runs your Go program for quick testing?
quizData.explanation: `go run` is the standard way to quickly test code during development.
